coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp
coredata.dc:creator: S., Thambiraj
D., Ravi Shankaran
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have great potential due to its advantageous characteristics of highly fluorescent nature and good stability. In this study, we aimed to develop a simple and efficient method for the green synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from sugarcane bagasse, a renewable and sustainable resource. The process involves the top down approach of chemical oxidation followed by exfoliation of sugarcane carbon. The synthesized CQDs was characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Spectrofluorophotometry, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The synthesized CQDs possess stable fluorescent properties, good bio-compatibility and high quantum yield. The CQDs are highly crystalline with longitudinal dimensions of 4.1±0.17nm with an average roughness of around 5nm. The XRD and TEM analysis indicates that the synthesized CQDs possess face centred cubic crystal structure. The results suggest that the proposed CQDs could be utilized for bio-sensor, bio-imaging and drug delivery applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Sugarcane bagasse pulp
Top down approach
Raman spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from sugarcane bagasse pulp carbon

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CQDs from sugarcane bagasse pulp.

HR-TEM Images of CQDs. (a) High magnification image, (b) mono dispersed image, (c) SAED pattern of CQDs, (d) Histogram image of CQDs with an average size of 4.1±0.17nm corresponding to image of (b), and (e) EDS image of CQDs, indicating the 87.18% of pure carbon.
XRD pattern of the synthesized CQDs, the shoulder peak observed at 2ϴ=20.6°.

AFM image of CQDs. Two dimensional image of CQDs, (a) Low magnification image of CQDs (5μm), (b) High magnification image of CQDs (2μm), (c) Three dimensional image of CQDs (corresponding image of b), and (d) histogram image of CQDs and the average size of ∼5nm.
Raman spectrum of synthesized CQDs and the spectrum were observed at the wavelength of 514nm.
The XPS spectrum of CQDs; (a) XPS survey spectrum, (b) binding energy spectrum of C 1s, (c) binding energy spectrum of O1s, and (d) FT-IR spectrum of synthesized CQDs.
Optical properties of CQDs (a) UV-vis spectrum of CQDs (λex=283nm) (b) Fluorescence spectrum of CQDs (λem=327 and 407nm).

Fluorescence properties of CQDs (a) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs at different wavelength (λex =283 and λem =300, 320, 340, 360, and 380nm) (b) Fluorescence microscope images of CQDs under UV-filter, (c) red filter and (d) green filter. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of the synthesis of CQDs from different precursor and synthesis routes.

row.entry: Imaging of bacterial and fungal Cells

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from sugarcane bagasse pulp

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano-Bio Materials and Sensors Laboratory, PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Coimbatore, 641 004, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai, 600 025, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Madras, Guindy Campus, Chennai, 600 025 Tamil Nadu, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of CQDs from sugarcane bagasse carbon

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have great potential due to its advantageous characteristics of highly fluorescent nature and good stability. In this study, we aimed to develop a simple and efficient method for the green synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from sugarcane bagasse, a renewable and sustainable resource. The process involves the top down approach of chemical oxidation followed by exfoliation of sugarcane carbon. The synthesized CQDs was characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Spectrofluorophotometry, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), Atomic force microscopy (AFM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The synthesized CQDs possess stable fluorescent properties, good bio-compatibility and high quantum yield. The CQDs are highly crystalline with longitudinal dimensions of 4.1±0.17nm with an average roughness of around 5nm. The XRD and TEM analysis indicates that the synthesized CQDs possess face centred cubic crystal structure. The results suggest that the proposed CQDs could be utilized for bio-sensor, bio-imaging and drug delivery applications.

ce:section.ce:para: There is an increasing interest in nanomaterials based innovations, especially the use of agricultural solid wastes as a source for the preparation of nanomaterials. Agricultural solid wastes are mainly found in the soil as a by-product from agriculture, industry and domestic purposes. It is known that the consequences related to pollution and environmental cleanup seem to be diverse. The agricultural solid wastes such as sugarcane bagasse, coir, banana and pineapple leaf etc., are cause of the major contamination and pollution of the soil and the earth [1]. In this aspect, we demonstrated an alternative method for reducing the pollution and contamination of the soil by manufacturing carbon materials using agricultural solid wastes [1,2]. Recently, many studies have been reported on the synthesis and characterisation of carbon based nanomaterials from solid wastes.

Sugarcane bagasse is one of the major solid wastes from agriculture. Efforts are being made to use for a large variety of applications such as bio-fuels, food products and carbon production [3,4]. Due to its abundance, low cost, biodegradable and recycling ability, sugarcane bagasse has been used for mass-production of carbon based materials as a natural precursor. Hence, it is a novel strategy for the development of efficient recycling the solid waste for both economic and sustainable aspects [5].

Carbon is one of the major and green elements on earth. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (luminescent semiconductor carbon nanoparticle) is most viable allotropes of carbon- based nanomaterials. CQDs are smaller in size and offers unique properties such as high photo stability [6,7] and quantum yield [8], broad absorption spectra [9], low-toxicity [10], strong fluorescent lifetime [11], ease of functionalization with organic molecules, emission tenability, biocompatibility and chemical inertness [12]. These properties have been exploited for various applications such as bio-sensing [13], bio-imaging [13–15], drug delivery [16], fluorescent probes [17], light emitting diodes [18], photocatalytic activity [19] and storage devices [20].

CQDs have been synthesized by various methods, including laser ablation [21], electrochemical method [22], hydrothermal method [23], microwave and ultrasonication method [24,25], oxidation of candle soot [11], wet chemical method [12], arc discharge [26] and microwave mediated synthesis [27]. Among them, chemical oxidation and exfoliations method were mainly carried out for the synthesis of CQDs from solid waste as a natural precursor. Nowadays, many researchers have been synthesizing CQDs from the natural precursor by using various synthesis routes. Table. 1 provides a detailed comparative study for the synthesis of CQDs from different materials and synthesis routes.

In this work, we developed a simple, low-cost and efficient method for synthesis of CQDs from sugarcane bagasse carbon. Here, CQDs were synthesized by the top-down approach of chemical oxidation followed by exfoliation method at room temperature with toluene as a solvent. The resulted CQDs possess bright fluorescence, good stability and high quantum yield. The purity of the synthesized CQDs was confirmed by using Raman spectroscopy (D/G ratio of 0.85). The synthesized fluorescent CQDs are spherical in shape with an average diameter of 4.1±017nm. The physicochemical properties of CQDs are highly promising for mass scale production, bio-sensor, bio-imaging and drug delivery applications.

Sugarcane bagasse pulp was purchased from a local market in Coimbatore. Toluene (≥99.9%) was obtained from Merck chemicals. Ethanol (99.9%) was purchased from Changsha Yangash chemicals. All solvents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Millipore water was used throughout the study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from sugarcane bagasse pulp carbon
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by modifying the procedure reported by Zhang et al., in 2013 [34]. The received sugarcane bagasse pulp was cut into small pieces and dried in sunlight for 6days prior to combustion of the pulp at 60°C in open atmosphere to form carbon. The yielded carbon was weighed 2g and added to 200mL of toluene and stirred thoroughly and continuously for 24h at room temperature for complete dispersion. Then, the dispersed carbon solution was ultra-sonicated for 1h and centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 30min at room temperature. After the removal of black precipitate, the supernatant liquid was collected. The supernatant liquid was then diluted with ethanol and used for further measurements. The schematic representation of the entire process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .
The synthesized CQDs from sugarcane bagasse pulp was characterized by using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques.

The morphological image of CQDs was obtained by High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) using JEOL-JEM 2010 instrument. A drop of CQDs solution was coated over the copper grid and the grid was dried in hot plate. The dried sample coated copper grid was analyzed with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) at are acceleration voltage of 200kV.

The crystallinity and phase purity of the synthesized CQDs was analyzed by using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. X-ray diffraction pattern was observed by using Shimadzu (XRD -6000, Japan) instrument. X-ray pattern equipped with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å) with the targeted voltage of 45kV and current of 40 mÅ.

AFM imaging was performed using a bench top Atomic force microscope (AFM) (Nanosurf, Switzerland) to analyze the surface topography. A small drop of CQDs was diluted with ethanol. The diluted sample was coated over freshly prepared UV-treated silicon wafer. CQDs coated silicon wafer was dried at room temperature. The dried sample was analyzed with the scanning rate of 0.5–1 lines for all images.

Raman spectrum was recorded using Horiba-Jobin, Lab Ram HR spectrometer. The sample was excited with the argon laser sources of 514.5nm with a resolution of <1.5cm−1. The synthesized CQDs solution was coated over freshly prepared glass plate and dried for 2h at room temperature. The spectrum was baseline corrected and maximizes the peak intensity.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements was performed using an Omicron Nanotechnology, ESCA-14 (Germany) instrument in the “hybrid” mode using a monochromatic and polychromatic Al Ka X-ray source and a nominal photoelectron take-off angle of 0° operated at 120W. For sample preparation, a small drop of CQDs solution was coated over freshly prepared glass substrate and dried at room temperature. Measurements were performed on three replicates for each sample. Data analysis was done using the Vision Processing data reduction software.

The surface functional groups were performed by using fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum was recorded with FT-IR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the spectral range of 400–4000cm−1 at room temperature.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded using Shimadzu (UV-1800), spectrophotometer. The sample was diluted in ethanol. The UV measurements were carried out in the absorption wavelength of 200nm to 600nm at room temperature.

The fluorescence spectrum was recorded with single beam spectrofluorophotometer, Shimadzu (Japan) at room temperature. The synthesized sample was diluted in ethanol. The diluted sample was recorded in different excitation wavelength (λex=283, 300, 320, 340, 360, and 380nm).

Fluorescence microscope images of synthesized CQDs was observed using Inverted epi-fluorescence microscope (Nikon-Germany). It was recorded at different wavelengths and with different filters. In the sample preparation, a small drop of CQDs solution was diluted with ethanol and sonicated for 5min. The diluted solution was coated over freshly prepared glass plate and dried at room temperature. The dried samples were analyzed in different filters ((UV) or blue filter, green and red filters).

CQDs were synthesized by extraction with toluene as a solvent. The HR-TEM image of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 . The high resolution image of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2a. The HR-TEM image clearly indicates that the CQDs are smaller in size, nearly spherical in shape and mono-dispersed (fig. 2b). The size distributions of the extracted carbon nanoparticles are illustrated in the histogram. The average size of the synthesized CQDs was found to be 4.1±0.17nm (fig. 2d). It is highly comparable to other reported work [21,54]. The crystalline nature of CQDs was studied by HR-TEM measurements using SAED pattern (fig. 2c). The crystal lattice spacing distances of CQDs is about 4.293Å. These results are in accordance with the (200), (311) diffraction planes of carbon (JCPDS 82-0505). Thus, the results indicates that the carbon dots are arranged in a face centred cubic crystals system with λ=1.5406Å.

Fig. 2e shows the EDS spectrum of synthesized CQDs. The EDS spectrum clearly indicates that 87.18wt.% of carbon is present in the sample. Here, the HR-TEM analysis suggests that the obtained CQDs exhibit graphite like structure [34]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first method to synthesize CQDs from sugarcane bagasse carbon and with the production of 87.18wt.% of high purity of the carbon.

The result of the XRD measurement of the synthesized CQDs is shown in Fig. 3 . The XRD characteristics peaks are appeared at 2ϴ=11.4°, 20.6°, 22.8°, 42.3°and 45.7°. The major peak of the CQDs was appeared at 2ϴ=20.6° and 42.3°. This is due to the presence of graphitic carbon [55]. A shoulder peak was observed at 20.6° corresponding to (311) plane of carbon. The weak peak observed at 42.3° is assigned to the plane of (533). In the CQDs, most intense peaks observed at 2ϴ=11.4°, 20.6° and weak peaks observed at 2ϴ=42.3° and 45.7° are corresponds to the plan of (200), (311), (533) and (711). This diffraction pattern completely matches with carbon corresponds to the JCPDF value of (82–0505) and hence the CQDs is a face centred cubic crystal. The shoulder peak at the interlayer spacing of ∼4.293Å is slightly higher than the spacing values between (311) plane is assigned for carbon (4.299Å) [2].

The topographical images were used for measuring the size and roughness of the synthesized CQDs. The AFM images of the synthesized CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 . It was observed that the CQDs were found to be very small in size and spherical in shape. The average diameters of the CQDs were found to be in the range of 3–5nm and the surface roughness is less than 5nm. The histogram of the CQDs in a ten counting scale showed an average roughness of 4.2nm. Fig. 4a illustrate the two dimensional image of the synthesised CQDs which was randomly arranged in the silicon wafer substrate at low magnification (5μm). The high magnification (2μm) images of CQDs were shown in Fig. 4b. Fig. 4c depicts the three dimensional image of CQDs observed with corresponding two dimensional images of Fig. 4b. Fig. 4d shows the histogram of CQDs indicating size in range of 3–5nm and surface roughness of 5nm. The observed size of the synthesized CQDs is smaller than the other reported works [2,31, 43, 51, 52 & 56]. Qu et al., 2012, reported CQDs with 6nm thickness, prepared from organic molecules (amino TPEA ([N-(2-aminoethyl)-N, N, N’-tris (pyridin-2-ylmethylethane-1, 2-diamine], AE-TPEA) [56].

The typical Raman spectrum of the synthesized CQDs is shown in Fig. 5 . The spectrum indicates the graphite nature of the carbon present in the synthesized CQDs. In the spectrum, the D-band and G-band were observed in the range of 1300–1400cm−1 and 1500–1650cm−1, respectively [57]. The sharp intense peak observed at 1580cm−1 of G-band and the weak peak observed at 1331cm−1 of D-band indicates the presence of carbon nanomaterials in the CQDs. The sharp intense peak of G-band at 1610cm−1 is the Raman active mode of graphite, which provides sp2 hybridization. The weak peak of D-band at 1361cm−1 reflects mainly (amorphous carbon) the amount of impurities present in the sample. The intensity ratio of the samples was calculated to be ID/IG =0.85 (sp3/sp2 carbon) which indicates 87% purity of the CQDs [58,59]. This result indicates better purity of the prepared CQDs compared to the other works [11,60]. The G and D-band intensities were obtained from their maximum peak counts of the spectrum [61]. The additional 2D-band peak was observed at 2854cm−1 of sp2 hybridization (2nd order phonon process) [2].

The surface composition and oxidation state of the synthesized CQDs were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The XPS survey spectra depict the two shoulder peaks at 287, 534eV corresponds to the K-shell characteristics peak of C1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 6 a). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s peaks was displayed that the two major peaks of 287.07 and 290.72eV which accords to sp2 carbon of the CO/CO and CC/CN bonds, respectively (Fig. 6b) [62–64]. High-resolution spectrum of the O1s element indicates the shoulder peak of 533.94 ascribed to the COH/COC groups [65,66] as shown in Fig. 2c. The graphite structure of the synthesized CQDs was confirmed by the binding energy peaks at 287.07eV corresponds to the C1s. The XPS results reveal that the surface of the oxygen-containing functionalized CQDs is possibly associated with carbonyl groups and hydroxyl functional groups.

The functional group and surface structure of the synthesized CQDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectrum. Fig. 6d indicates strong absorption bands at 2985cm−1 and 2813cm−1 associated with CC stretching vibration of methyl or methylene groups present in the CQDs with toluene. A weak absorption band appeared at 3440cm−1 associated to the presence of hydrogen bonds, and that of 1728cm−1 is due to the presence of carbonyl group (CO). These functional groups indicate the functionality of the CQDs having CH3 and OH groups, which could be used for drug delivery and sensor applications [34,67].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the solution containing CQDs is shown in Fig. 7 a. The typical absorption peak was observed at 283nm. The CQDs solution exhibits a sharp absorption peak around 283nm which is attributed to the π-π* transition for aromatic sp2 hybridisation. The quantum yield of the CQDs extracted from organic solvent is approximately 18.7 % (λex=283nm) with excitation wavelength of 283nm. Peng and Travas-Sejdic., 2009 reported that the surface passivation for the CQDs extracted with organic solvent provides better quantum yield [68].

The CQDs extracted with toluene showed an excitation wavelength at 283nm and emission peaks at 327, 338, 408 and 438nm as shown in Fig. 7(b). The emission spectrum indicates the high fluorescence nature of the synthesized CQDs because of its higher surface area, quantum effect, radiative property and emissive traps [69,70]. However, the organic solvents play an important role for attached with carbon particles and improved the fluorescence property. The results are highly comparable with the excitation wavelengths of 305 and 385nm observed with the CQDs prepared from polystyrene foam waste soot [34]. Moreover, the fluorescence study was carried out at different excitation wavelengths (fig. 8 a) and was observed that the excitation wavelength increases with the increase in the emission intensity [56].

Li et al., (2012) reported, the CQDs showing a broad absorption peak of 300nm are ascribed to the high energy level and the complicated band structure [71]. Fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs observed at different excitation wavelengths with light ranging from 283 to 380nm. The fluorescence spectra exhibit the blue shift, which increases with the increase in the excitation wavelength. The increasing excitation peak observed at 300nm which shows the strongest fluorescence appears in the synthesized CQDs (Fig. 8a). Tang et al., (2012) reported, the CQDs observed in the fluorescence spectra mainly emit blue, green and red emission. However, the emissions are better than the values observed by their study [72].

The fluorescence microscopic studies reveals that the high florescence nature of CQDs with different bandwidths. The particles were seen with blue emission under UV-filter, red emission under green filter and green emission under the red filter as shown in Fig. 8b–d. It is expected that this wide range of fluorescence emission is highly advantageous and efficient compared to CQDs with single green fluorescence [2]. The results suggests that the present CQDs could be a good alternative to tradition dyes for fluorescent labelling, bio-imaging and bio-sensor applications [11].

We have developed a green, simple and efficient method for synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from sugarcane bagasse using chemical oxidation and exfoliation. The synthesized CQDs are highly fluorescence in nature and possess good stability. It showed broad UV–vis absorption spectrum and excitation wavelength with independent fluorescent behaviour with high quantum yield approximately 18.7%. The Raman and EDS spectral studies demonstrate that the synthesized CQDs were found to be 87% purity. The HR-TEM and AFM studies indicate the crystalline nature of the CQDs with a size of 4.1±0.17nm and surface thickness of 5nm. The SAED pattern and XRD peaks of synthesized CQDs indicates the face centred cubic crystal structures. The highly crystalline and fluorescent CQDs provide good potential for bio-sensors, bio-medical imaging, drug delivery and solar cell applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to PSG Institute of Advance Studies for providing the facilities to carry out this research work.

ation of carbon quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence by rapid laser passivation in ordinary organic solvents
